title: Basics of RSA
summary: I've spent the last week or so writing a plugin for my website to render LaTeX without any client-side Javascript. As a demo, I'm publishing my high-school paper on RSA. content:
I've spent the last week or so writing a plugin for my website to render LaTeX without any client-side Javascript. As a demo, I'm publishing my high-school paper on RSA.

Cryptography is the foundation of modern online communications. Without it, online banking information, private data, and much more could be intercepted and read by anyone with the skills to do so. With the advent of a mathematical algorithm called RSA (standing for the last names of the authors: Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman) in 1978, communication and secrecy was changed forever. It is now possible to make it mathematically difficult to break encryption but incredibly simple to encrypt something. Public key cryptography and RSA is the reason why it is difficult for cybercriminals to see what websites you visit, your bank information as it is transmitted over the internet, and the password to your Facebook account. Public key cryptography and RSA have fundamentally changed communication.

Modern asymmetric cryptography (also known as public key cryptography or private key cryptography) relies on two parties generating two linked “keys”: a public key, which can be shared with everyone, and a private key, which must be kept secret. Using the example in the original RSA study, if Alice wants to send a document to Bob, then Alice will encrypt that document with Bob’s public key, and Bob can decrypt that document with his own private key. It is possible to derive the private key from the public key, but it is computationally difficult to do so. Assuming an RSA cryptosystem with a key length of 4096 bits (the number in binary is 4096 digits long), it is possible to estimate how long it would take to brute-force this key. According to the prime number theorem, there are roughly

xln⁡x

primes up to x . Therefore, with a prime length of exactly 2048 bits (modern implementations vary the prime length by a few bits to make this even more computationally difficult), there are

22049ln⁡22049−22048ln⁡22048=2.2743×10613

possible keys. Therefore, the number of distinct prime pairs ( p and q , required for RSA key generation) is

(2.2743×10613)22−2.2743×10613=2.5863×101226

pairs. Assuming that every atom in the observable universe (roughly 1080 ) checks one pair per nanosecond ( 10−9 seconds), it would take

2.5863×101226ns1080processors=2.5863×101146ns

or 8.19555×101129 years. For reference, the universe is about 1010 years old. Although there are algorithms that exist that can factor the public key much faster (such as Shor’s algorithm and quantum computing technology, or the general number field sieve using typical classical computing approaches), it is still not feasible for these key lengths for the foreseeable future.

The principle of RSA encryption is that it is computationally difficult to factor numbers. If two very large primes are multiplied together, it is difficult to find the original two numbers from the product. However, it has not yet been proven whether a classical algorithm can factor numbers in polynomial time1 exists or does not exist. Mathematician Peter Shor discovered an algorithm in 1994 that can factor numbers in polynomial time, but it only works on a large quantum computer. According to Martin Roetteler, a quantum computing scientist at Microsoft, it would take " 2n+2 qubits which leads to a quantum circuit implementation that has less than 448n3log2⁡n number of T-gates. For a bit-size of n=1024 , this would work out to be 2050 logical qubits and 4.81×1012 T-gates." As of the end of 2021, the largest quantum computer in the world, IBM’s Eagle Quantum computer has 127 logical qubits. If Rose’s Law2 is applicable here, it would take

5(log2⁡2050−log2⁡127)=20years

to reach the number of qubits needed to break a weak 1,024 bit RSA key. Hopefully, the internet has moved to a stronger form of encryption by then.

Theoretical RSA is vastly different from applied RSA. Applied RSA has many additions to theoretical (or “book”) RSA that would make it more difficult to break. Since many messages can start with and end with predictable phrases and words (such as starting with “Dear” and ending with “Sincerely yours”), it would be possible to find the key from that information. There is much more that is done additional to standard RSA that is outside the scope of this paper including increasing the entropy (making the plaintext, or original message, seem more random to prevent frequency attacks), and establishing a chain of trust to prevent Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks.

The easiest way to grasp RSA is by an analogy of paint: it is easy to mix together two paint colors but it is difficult to unmix them. Assume that Alice wants to send a private message to Bob. However, Eve wants to know what is being said. Alice and Bob must generate a secret “shared key”, or password, to encrypt their information with. Eve must not discover the password. Alice and Bob can generate a shared color using paint using the following steps:

  1. Alice and Bob agree on a shared base color. This is allowed to be public.
  2. Alice and Bob both pick a random color as their personal key. This is the “private key” and must be kept secret.
  3. Alice and Bob each mix their private key and the base color to generate their public keys.
  4. The public keys are swapped. Eve now has the base color and both public keys.
  5. Alice and Bob add their own private keys to the other’s public key. They now have a shared secret color. Eve can not find this color because she does not have access to either private key.

This example with paint is not trivial to convert into something computers can use. However, Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman published how to do so in 1978, changing communication forever3. The process of generating a shared secret number is similar to the paint experiment, but still not trivial to discover:

  1. Alice and Bob each generate two large prime numbers p and q . p and q must differ by a few orders of magnitude to make factorization by Fermat’s factorization method4 difficult.
  2. Alice and Bob each generate and share n=pq . This is the public key.
  3. Alice and Bob each calculate Carmichael’s totient function of n as λ(n) .
  4. Alice and Bob both agree on an integer e such that e<λ(n) and e is coprime to λ(n) .
  5. Alice and Bob each calculate d as being the modular multiplicative inverse of e modulo λ(n) .

Once these values are generated and n and e are shared, Alice and Bob can encrypt messages to each other that can only be decrypted by the other person. If Alice wishes to send a message to Bob, she would take her message and turn it into a number. This is possible to do so by converting the message into binary then into an integer. Then, Alice will split the message up so that each chunk is less than n . Finally, Alice will encrypt the message m with Bob’s public key n and the shared exponent e :

c≡memodn

Alice can transmit the ciphertext c to Bob, and Bob can obtain the original message m by solving for m:

m≡cdmodn

With the information transmitted, Eve—who is only listening to the communication between Alice and Bob—can not easily find p and q, and thus can not decrypt the message.

A practical example would be Bob attempting to send Alice a secret number: 1234. Alice and Bob would both first generate their required values:

Value Alice Bob
p 74177 13613
q 99817 99817
n 74177×99817=7404125609 13613×66293=902446609
λ(n) 7403951616 902366704
e 127 127
d 127−1mod7403951616=2623447423 127−1mod902366704=412104479

Now, Bob would encrypt 1234 with Alice’s public key and e:

1234127mod7403951616=6062692598

Bob’s secret message is c=6062692598 . Alice would receive that message and decrypt it like so:

60626925982623447423mod7404125609=1234

In real life, p and q would be much larger, e would typically be 65537, and the message would be first compressed to increase entropy then encrypted with a shared password that was agreed upon with RSA (since it is faster to share a secret password then encrypt the message using that password). However, this demonstration shows the encryption and decryption process using RSA with about 40 bits of strength. RSA has also been proved in the original paper to be correct with Fermat’s little theorem5. Proving

(me)d≡mmodn

for any integer m where n is the product of two primes p and q , and e and d are positive integers that are the modular multiplicative inverse of Carmichael’s totient function is trivial.

Since Carmichael’s totient function is defined as

λ(pq)=lcm(p−1,q−1)

then it is divisible by both p−1 and q−1 .

Since λ(pq) is divisible by both p−1 and q−1 , then

ed−1=h(p−1)=k(q−1)

where h and k are two positive integers. This statement is true for any e and d that satisfy

ed≡1mod((p−1)(q−1))

because (p−1)(q−1) is divisible by λ(pq) , and therefore also by p−1 and q−1 .

By the properties of the modulus operator, checking if a≡bmodpq is equivalent to checking if a≡bmodp and a≡bmodq separately.

Given that m is not zero,

med≡mmodp

then,

med=med−1×m=mh(p−1)×m=(mp−1)h×m

By Fermat's Little Theorem,

(mp−1)h×m≡1h×m≡mmodp

If m is zero, then a special case occurs. If m is congruent to 0modp , then m is a multiple of p .

Therefore, med is also a multiple of p . The logical conclusion for that special case of m=0 is the following:

med≡0≡m

Proving that med proceeds in the same way as proving that med≡mmodp . Therefore, for any positive integer m , and positive integers e , d such that ed≡1modλ(pq) , then med≡mmodpq .

Understanding RSA is essential for understanding how the internet operates securely. The advent of RSA has revolutionized modern communication. Because it is computationally difficult to factor a large semiprimes6 but it is very easy to multiply large numbers together, it is difficult to break an RSA key but it is easy to generate one. Because it is near impossible to break a long RSA key with the equipment currently available, then malicious actors must move to alternate ways to break encryption. From the US Government’s backdoor (and frontdoor) efforts against cryptography, such as lobbying to reduce key sizes, banning the export of cryptography software, arresting software developers, and so on and so forth, to the movement away from encrypted communications platforms toward centralized ones for the sake of convenience (such as Discord and Slack), it seems that RSA—and all other forms of encryption—are only as strong as those who implement it.

Sources

Briggs, Matthew Edward. An Introduction to the General Number Field Sieve. Virginia Tech, 17 Apr. 1998.

Cocks, C. C.. “A note on non-secret encryption.” (1973).

Dean, Walter. “Computational Complexity Theory.” The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, edited by Edward N. Zalta, Fall 2021, Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University, 2021.

Doctorow, Cory. “Apple vs FBI: The Privacy Disaster Is Inevitable, but We Can Prevent the Catastrophe.” Craphound.Com, https://craphound.com/news/2016/03/04/apple-vs-fbi-the-privacy-disaster-is-inevitable-but-we-can-prevent-the-catastrophe/. Accessed 18 Jan. 2022.

Editor, Csrc Content. “Man-in-the-Middle Attack (MitM) - Glossary.” Nist.Gov, https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary/term/man_in_the_middle_attack. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Graeme. “Understanding Public Key Cryptography with Paint – modulo Errors.” Straylight.Co.Uk, https://maths.straylight.co.uk/archives/108. Accessed 11 Feb. 2022.

“How Many Logical Qubits Are Needed to Run Shor’s Algorithm Efficiently on Large Integers ( n>21024 )?” Quantum Computing Stack Exchange, https://quantumcomputing.stackexchange.com/questions/5048/how-many-logical-qubits-are-needed-to-run-shors-algorithm-efficiently-on-large/5056. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

“IBM Unveils Breakthrough 127-Qubit Quantum Processor.” IBM Newsroom, https://newsroom.ibm.com/2021-11-16-IBM-Unveils-Breakthrough-127-Qubit-Quantum-Processor. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Rivest, R. L., et al. “A Method for Obtaining Digital Signatures and Public-Key Cryptosystems.” Communications of the ACM, vol. 21, no. 2, 1978, pp. 120–126, doi:10.1145/359340.359342.

Shor, Peter W. “Polynomial-Time Algorithms for Prime Factorization and Discrete Logarithms on a Quantum Computer.” SIAM Journal on Computing, vol. 26, no. 5, 1997, pp. 1484–1509, doi:10.1137/s0097539795293172.

Sullivan, Nick. “A (Relatively Easy To Understand) Primer on Elliptic Curve Cryptography.” Cloudflare.Com, 23 Oct. 2013, https://blog.cloudflare.com/a-relatively-easy-to-understand-primer-on-elliptic-curve-cryptography/.

Wagner, Lane. “What Is Entropy in Cryptography?” Qvault, 28 Sept. 2020, https://qvault.io/cryptography/what-is-entropy-in-cryptography/.

Weisstein, Eric W. “Carmichael Function.” Https://Mathworld.Wolfram.Com/, https://mathworld.wolfram.com/CarmichaelFunction.html. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Weisstein, Eric W. “Fermat’s Factorization Method.” Https://Mathworld.Wolfram.Com/, https://mathworld.wolfram.com/FermatsFactorizationMethod.html. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Weisstein, Eric W. “Fermat’s Little Theorem.” Https://Mathworld.Wolfram.Com/, https://mathworld.wolfram.com/FermatsLittleTheorem.html. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Weisstein, Eric W. “Prime Number Theorem.” Https://Mathworld.Wolfram.Com/, https://mathworld.wolfram.com/PrimeNumberTheorem.html. Accessed 12 Jan. 2022.

Yoder, Nicholas. “Moore’s Law of Moore’s Law of Quantum Computing.” Nick Yoder, 15 Apr. 2021, https://nickyoder.com/moores-law-quantum-computer/.

  1. Computational complexity describes the amount of computer time to run an algorithm. Polynomial time is denoted in Big-O notation: O(bk) , where k is some integer. Big-O notation describes the behavior of the function as it approaches infinity. As the size of the input of the algorithm approaches infinity, the O-notation approaches some function: f(x)=O(g(x)) as x approaches infinity, where f is a real or complex valued function and g is a real-valued function. Functions can scale linearly with the input ( O(n) ), which means that as the input size increases, then the time also increases linearly. The “best” O-notation could be O(1) , where regardless of input, the algorithm takes the same time to perform computations. Likewise, O(n!) would be disastrously slow as the input size increases. ↩

  2. Considered the "Moore’s Law of Quantum Computers" and represents the growth of quantum computers over time. ↩

  3. British mathematician Clifford Cocks discovered the same cryptosystem earlier in 1973 while working for the GCHQ (the British signal intelligence agency). It was kept a secret until it was declassified in 1997. ↩

  4. Fermat’s factorization method works on the basis that odd integers can be represented in the format n=a2−b2=(a+b)(a−b) , where (a+b) and (a−b) are factors of the number n . Prime numbers are odd, so Fermat’s method is applicable here. Taking the square root of n can determine p and q easily if p and q are close together. ↩

  5. If p is a prime number, then for any integer a , the number ap−a is a multiple of p . If a is not divisible by p , Fermat’s little theorem states that ap−1≡1modp . ↩

  6. Two prime numbers multiplied together ↩
